turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: We will hear argument next in Case 12ﾂｭ1173, Marvin Brandt Revocable Trust v. United States. Mr. Lechner.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: If upheld, the decision below will upset 100 years of property law and title to perhaps a million acres of land, based upon an implied reversionary interest that the government recently created. I would like to make three points: First, the decision below violates Leo Sheep, where this Court rejected the government's attempt to create a property interest through implication and reaffirmed that the government does not retain any interest not expressly reserved in the patent or the granting statute. Two, the decision below is contrary to both the government's argument and this Court's ruling in Great Northern that 1875 Act rightsﾂｭofﾂｭway are easements and are not fees. And finally, the decision below is inconsistent with the Department of Interior's longstanding interpretation that the 1875 Act granted only an easement. It is axiomatic that the highest evidence of title in this country is a patent from the government. When the government issues a patent, it divests itself of title except for those interests expressly reserved. Here, the patent did not reserve any interest in the 1875 Act ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Counsel, you are not reading this, are you?

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: It's all right.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Here the patent did not reserve any interest in 1875 Act rightﾂｭofﾂｭway. Yet the government knew how to reserve interests at that time because it reserved the interest for ditches and canal, and reserved the right to two Forest Service roads when it issued the patent. That some interests were reserved by the government in 1976 shows that other interests were not.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, under Section 1912, the government could ﾂｭﾂｭ did reserve an interest for a public highway; is that correct?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: No?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: 1912, Section 1912 has to be read in conjunction with this Court's decision in Northern Pacific v. Townsend, and Stringham. And in those decisions, this Court held that railroad rightsﾂｭofﾂｭway were limited fees with implied reversionary interests.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: The patent you talk about, I am not recalling it exactly. It had some ﾂｭﾂｭ it mentioned the railroad interest, right?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Yes, it mentioned it in the context of, that Brandt's title is subject to those rights for railroad purposes as had been granted to the railroad under the 1875 Act.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: So it didn't characterize it as either an easement or a fee with an implied reverter?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: That it was subject to those rights for railroad purposes as had been granted to the railroad sounds like it was granted subject to a servitude. "Rights for railroad purpose" sounds like a servitude. And of course that's how the Department of Interior interpreted it at the time, that these 1875 rightsﾂｭofﾂｭway were easements, following its longstanding interpretation plus this Court's ruling in Great Northern that these were easements. And at the time, that regulation was the law of the land. And that's ﾂｭﾂｭ that controlled the title that Brandt got.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: What they are saying, I think the government's point is ﾂｭﾂｭ the other side is saying, and I don't remember the term of my property law. What's the right term? It's ﾂｭﾂｭ you grant to A, black acre to A and his heirs; it's a fee simple. But it's subject to a shifting; it's subject to divestiture, subject to a condition subsequent. What's the right term?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Well ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: It shifts the ﾂｭﾂｭ it shifts it back.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: A defeasible fee? Or implied conditional ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: It could be. There was a technical term they used to have. But anyway, that's what they're saying.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: That's what they're saying.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: And they are saying that that's, for purposes of who owns the land, the mineral rights, it's treated as an easement. But for purposes of deciding who it reverts to, it's treated as a fee subject to a shifting use or whatever you call it. Subject to ﾂｭﾂｭ what did you say? Subject to ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Subject to a right of reverter.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Yes. But that's similar to the argument that the government made in 1942 in Great Northern. The limited fee ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, it's different in this respect. It's different that there they were talking about who owns the mineral rights. So for those purposes they say it's treated like an easement. But for who gets the reverter, it's treated like a shift of the fee.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Oh, that's nice. Do you know of any other real estate thing which says it's an easement for one purpose and the fee for another? The government absolutely denied in Great Northern, didn't it? It was ﾂｭﾂｭ that was the government's case; this was not a fee.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Exactly.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: They didn't say it's a ﾂｭﾂｭ you know, for some purposes it is. They said it was not a fee. And that's what the Court held, contrary ﾂｭﾂｭ in accordance with what the government wanted, right?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And that case has been around for how long?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: 72 years.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Okay. And the case explicitly says it's an easement. And people have been buying and selling real estate I assume in reliance on that case for ﾂｭﾂｭ for that entire period of time. And now the government has this new theory that for some purposes it's a fee; for some purposes it's an easement. That's not what Great Northern said.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: No, but I think it's important that ﾂｭﾂｭ the argument that the government is making today is similar to their alternative argument that they made there in Great Northern, saying, okay, well, if it's not an easement, then it's a limited fee in the surface. This Court in Great Northern did not even address that alternative argument, which shows how strongly this Court in Great Northern believed it was just an easement. And this ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: But they are not really not alternative ﾂｭﾂｭ well, maybe it's all right to say they are alternative arguments, but they are mutually exclusive arguments. I mean they are alternative in the sense that they led to the same result, but the one argument is that this is A, and the other argument is this is not A.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Yes. They窶囘 be mutually exclusive.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: All right. We have a brief here from the cities, and they point out that ﾂｭﾂｭ that, of course, there is reliance on your side; you're ﾂｭﾂｭ I believe you are absolutely right. But they say there is also a lot of reliance on the other side, because cities have built highways, and they've ﾂｭﾂｭ or states have, and they've converted it to use under patents or under statutes that the United States gave them that right. And all those are going to be invalid if you win. So what's your response to that?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: They would all ﾂｭﾂｭ I'm not sure that they would be invalid, necessarily.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Why ﾂｭﾂｭ why?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Well, I'm not ﾂｭﾂｭ I don't know where all the city public highways' interests are, visﾂｭﾃﾂｭvis how the land was originally patented. It could have been originally patented in a town site plat or something like that, that ﾂｭﾂｭ as opposed to here, where it was originally patented to one ﾂｭﾂｭ under one patent, and I'm not sure how all the cities' property was acquired.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I assume your answer would be that if it's a choice between disappointing people who properly relied on the law, and disappointing people who were ﾂｭﾂｭ were not following the law, it's clear who should ﾂｭﾂｭ who should take it in the neck. It's the latter, isn't it?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Absolutely, plus the cities also have the ﾂｭﾂｭ the power of the eminent ﾂｭﾂｭ the power of eminent domain. And if they would want a public highway, they certainly could condemn one.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: I thought this ﾂｭﾂｭ the cities were relying on Section 912, as applied to the 1875 Act.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: That's what ﾂｭﾂｭ that's what they ﾂｭﾂｭ that's what the cities were relying on. But you must keep in mind that Section 912 did not alter or amend the 1875 Act, so the nature of the grant in the 1875 Act must be looked through the eyes of Congress in 1875. In 1922, when Congress passed Section 912, Congress was grappling with what to do with these isolated strips of land that would be administratively burdensome to manage. And they said ﾂｭﾂｭ well, originally, they said, well, let's give them to the settlers because they are the ones entitled to it in the first place, and then ﾂｭﾂｭ and then as an afterthought said, well, let's put these as a public highway. But they did not amend the grant under the 1875 Act or alter the amended grant.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: Well, can you explain to me something to me about that 1875 Act a little bit? We know that until 1871, these railroad rightsﾂｭofﾂｭway were considered to leave the government in the position of having a reversionary interest.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: And I don't see anything in the 1875 Act statute. There was an end to the giving away of large ﾂｭﾂｭ large quantities of land to the railroad, and so that was out. But what is there in the 1875 Act that changes the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway from what it had been up until 1871?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: As the government argued in Great Northern and as this Court adopted that argument, the key provision in the 1875 Act is Section 4, which reserved the right to Congress to dispose of the lands underlying 1875 rightsﾂｭofﾂｭway to settlers. And this was a reflection of the change of policy in 1871 because that's where the Congress changed its policy to ﾂｭﾂｭ in an effort to secure homesteads to actual settlers as opposed to benefiting the railroads. In Section 4, as this Court said in Great Northern, after language to demonstrate a conveyance of an easement would be hard to find.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: But the railroad wasn't benefited by the end of the 1871 understanding of the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway. The one that was benefited was the government. The government got ﾂｭﾂｭ got back the land, so it's not ﾂｭﾂｭ we're not ﾂｭﾂｭ was going to stop giving land to the railroad, yes, but the beneficiary that is losing out under your interpretation is the government.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: But it's ﾂｭﾂｭ it's not only my interpretation, it's the interpretation of the Court in Great Northern that it was an easement. And this Court was not ﾂｭﾂｭ this Court in Great Northern knew the difference of the word "easement" and knew what the significance of using that term would mean. So did the government at the time.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: There were ﾂｭﾂｭ there were points along the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway where the railroad needed a station or a wider area for water towers and so forth. And it ﾂｭﾂｭ it usually could receive those by simply filing a patent if it was within a permitted zone, if it was within ﾂｭﾂｭ if they were 20 miles equidistant or something like that. How did they get those additional lands?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: The station ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: One way they could do them was to condemn them, but absent condemnation, could they just receive them from the government?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Well, under ﾂｭﾂｭ under the 1875 Act, yes, Congress did provide for station grounds. The railroad could secure station grounds by filing a map of the station grounds with the local land office, and once the secretary approved, then they would acquire the station grounds. But the nature of the grant that they required on the station grounds is the same as for the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway, and that's merely an easement.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Oh. So it's ﾂｭﾂｭ so it was clear at the time of the grant that it was on the ﾂｭﾂｭ that what was granted was coﾂｭextensive with what the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway was, with the railroad rightﾂｭofﾂｭway.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Yes. And that was how the Department of Interior originally interpreted it in 1888, as the ﾂｭﾂｭ the railroad didn't get a fee in anything, it got a rightﾂｭofﾂｭway ﾂｭﾂｭ it got an easement in the railroad ﾂｭﾂｭ in the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway and in the station grounds.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Are there any instances in which the railroad can keep its station even if it abandons the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway? Or does the station fall with the ﾂｭﾂｭ when the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway is abandoned?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: I think the right ﾂｭﾂｭ the station grounds are tied to the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway. So if you abandon the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway, the station grounds would also be extinguished and unburden the underlying fee.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Could I ask you again about Section 912? Your patent says that it is subject to those rights for railroad purposes as have been granted to the railway company in particular, and its successors and assigns. Now, that was ﾂｭﾂｭ that patent came after the enactment of 912, and 912 clarifies the rights of ﾂｭﾂｭ or changes the rights of railroad companies and says that if they abandon property, within one year after that, they ﾂｭﾂｭ there can be a public highway established on that ﾂｭﾂｭ on that property. So why wasn't your patent subject to that?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Well ﾂｭﾂｭ well, first, I don't ﾂｭﾂｭ 912 did not change the grant to the railroad. 912 applies if the government has any remaining interest in the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, you didn't get your property from the railroad. You got it from the government.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: You got a patent from the government. So the question is: What were they conveying to you? And if you read the patent in connection with 912, isn't it clear they're conveying to you every ﾂｭﾂｭ arguably everything other than this right to have a public highway established on that land when it was abandoned by the railroad?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: They were conveying it subject to those rights for railroad purposes is the specific language. And but 912 does not create a reservation; it doesn't create a right of reverter. It was simply to deal with what they were going to do with these strips of land upon abandonment. Because at the time, that's when abandonment of the railroads first began, Congress was worried we were going to have these isolated strips. It didn't change the 1875 Act, it didn't change the previously granted rightsﾂｭofﾂｭway to expand the scope of them. But when they were abandoned and they came back under the law at the time that the government had the implied condition of reverter, they came back. The government said, okay, well, if you want to establish a public highway, that's fine, otherwise, it's going to go ﾂｭﾂｭ it's going to inure to the benefit of the settler. But it didn't change the nature of the grant. It didn't change the 1875 Act. It didn't create it. It did not create an interest that wasn't there before. It was just how to deal with the interest that the ﾂｭﾂｭ that Congress thought it had after this Court's decision in Townsend and Stringham.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: I still don't understand it. You say this was purely an easement. All right. So that means that when there's an abandonment, the government has complete title to the property, right? They convey that property to you, but before they do that, they say that there's this reservation of rights with respect to the establishment of a highway.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: I'm ﾂｭﾂｭ I'm ﾂｭﾂｭ one ﾂｭﾂｭ well, I'm ﾂｭﾂｭ I'm troubled. Under ﾂｭﾂｭ under your hypothetical, the government still owns the underlying land when the railroad's abandoned? So when the railroad is abandoned, the two estates merge. There was one.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: That's your argument, isn't it?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Well, I think that's the argument under ﾂｭﾂｭ under the common law, too, is ﾂｭﾂｭ is what ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Yes. Okay. And may be ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: So they ﾂｭﾂｭ they merge.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: It may be a good argument.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: They merge.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: But that's your argument.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Now, what about when 912 says that when the railroad abandons a piece of the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway, okay, it then says that the interest of the United States, with all right, title, and interest in the state of the United States shall be vested in the town that builds the highway. But your point is, that's true, but where they previously ﾂｭﾂｭ where they have previous ﾂｭﾂｭ where they have given ﾂｭﾂｭ where they conveyed it to a private person, they didn't have any right, title, and interest, because on abandonment it was simply an easement. And on abandonment, that goes to the property owner who owns the ﾂｭﾂｭ the land on either side. Is that ﾂｭﾂｭ I mean, your point is that the United States didn't have any right, title, and interest on the abandonment unless the United States continued to own the property.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: All right. So that is your point. They can't convey what they don't have.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: And there's ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Let's take this in a normal easement situation. I have an easement to go through your backyard to get to the street. Can I assign that easement to another person so that the person who buys my home can now walk through your backyard?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: In certain circumstances, yes.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So what's wrong with the concept that when this land was given to you as a railroad rightﾂｭofﾂｭway, when it's abandoned, that the railroad, in essence, under 1912, and under the 1922 Act, that you are giving that easement to the U.S. to use as a rightﾂｭofﾂｭway? And so, whether it's a trail or a highway or any of the other items that are specified in the statute that that easement is continuing to be used by the person who gave it, which was originally the U.S. Your patent was given subject to that easement, to that rightﾂｭofﾂｭway.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: It was subject to those rights for railroad purposes, correct.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well, what was the language of the patent?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Subject to those rights for railroad purposes, that conveys ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Was that the exact language? I thought it was for rightﾂｭofﾂｭway.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Exact language on page 78, Petitioner's Appendix.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The Joint Appendix?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Petitioner's Appendix, 78, sorry.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Too many briefs. Sorry.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: And what do you want us to read there?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Well, there was a question about whether that was actually the language, was it subject to those rights. The last full paragraph, "subject to those rights for railroad purposes have been granted to the railroad under the 1875 Act."

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The 1875 Act, what was given to the railroad was a rightﾂｭofﾂｭway.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: A right to lay their tracks and a right of passage.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Just as I'm seeing the 1875 grant, it was the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway through public lands.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Well, yes, and as the government argued in Great Northern that that was ﾂｭﾂｭ that right in the 1875 Act was a right to lay tracks and it was a right of passage, an easement.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Lechner, I know Great Northern said so and that is surely very strong support for your view. But why did Great Northern think that there was a difference between preﾂｭ1871 grants and post?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: First, the shift in Congress's policy. And the railroads fell out of disfavor and Congress passed resolution saying ﾂｭﾂｭ you know, we want to hold on to these lands to secure homesteads for actual settlers.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I mean, I guess I had thought if you were just dealing with this as a matter of first impression that the shift in Congress's policy was a shift about whether to give away ﾂｭﾂｭ you know, huge swaths of land to the railroads, and that Congress indeed decided, enough of this, we are not going to give these checkerboard grants of land to the railroad, because they are making a mint from this, and we would rather give it to homesteaders. But there was ﾂｭﾂｭ I don't see any evidence that there was any shift in policy or any reason why there would be a shift in policy, as to what the rights of way were. Whether they were easements or whether they were limited fees.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Well, Section 4 has a lot to do with that, and that is where Congress reserved the right to dispose of the underlying lands to the settlers. That provision is not found in the preﾂｭ1871 railroad grants, and this Court in Great Northern looked at that and you say, you got to look at Section 4, that provides light on how to interpret the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway grant in Section 1. And because of Congress's intent and the legislative history surrounding similar language when Congress passed it in 1872, they called it the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway is merely going to be an encumbrance.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I guess ﾂｭﾂｭ you know, again, if you were just looking at this as a matter of first impression, the language of Section 4 does not seem to me to be very indicative of anything. You know, it says "subject to the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway." Subject to the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway. Whatever the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway is. Subject to the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway if it's an easement. Subject to the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway if it's a limited fee. Doesn't seem to pick one or the other.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: But as the government argued in Great Northern, it would be silly to patent the underlying lands subject to a fee. I mean, there would be no reason for that language at all if the railroad got a fee. And then you patented the ﾂｭﾂｭ in effect it would be the adjacent land, not the underlying land. There would be no reason to say that the adjacent land is subject to a fee because with the limited fees, they were envisioning those things going to the center of the earth. So you would be patenting the adjacent land. But under Section 4 and as Great Northern interpreted, the patents go to the underlying land and that's what happened in this case, because Brandt owns the underlying land and minerals.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, in your answer it seemed to me, and maybe you have to do this to proffer, that you confused limited fee with fee simple ,with absolute fee. Now, obviously that's true if it's the same owner, they merge.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Well, I was using limited fee in the context as this Court used it in Townsend, limited fee with the implied condition of reverter. I mean, even in Townsend 1903, the railroad got it all, but it was subject to implied condition of reverter if they stopped using it for railroad purpose. But they did give fee simple absolute to the center of the earth is how it was construed at that time. So that sheds light on the fact that Section 4 reflects that only an easement was granted because you would not need to have Subject 2 language at all if you were just patenting the adjacent lands. It is also well established that the Department of Interior窶冱 interpretation of public land laws such as the 1875 Act is entitled to great deference. This is because public land laws provide for the acquisition of the title, which must be secure. Beginning in 1888 and continuing through today, the Department of Interior still construes the 1875 Act as easements and they can still construe 1875 Act rights of way as different from preﾂｭ1871.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I don't understand how that can be, and yet, the government can argue contrary to what the government says. I mean, is that what you are telling us?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: I'm saying ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: The Interior says one thing and the Justice Department here says something else? I mean, we have a president who reconciles these two things. He must agree with one or the other.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Well, I think, under this Court's precedent, you defer to the agency in charge of administering the 1875 Act, and that is the Department of Interior. And the Department of Interior has consistently interpreted these as ﾂｭﾂｭ these rights of way as easements and that interpretation is entitled to substantial deference.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I'm sure the government will have an explanation as to why we shouldn't.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: If it pleases the Court, I would like to reserve the remainder of my time for rebuttal.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Yang.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The statute, its legislative history, the surrounding statutory context which includes Section 912 as well as 1906 and 1909 Acts and this Court's decisions construing the 1875 Act including Section 4 which predated Great Northern show that the United States retains the reversionary interest in the surface of the land of rightﾂｭofﾂｭways granted to railroads ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Mr. Yang, I have to say I think the government gets a prize for understatement with its brief in this case. You have a sentence in your brief that says, "We acknowledge that there is language in this Court's opinion in Great Northern and in the government's brief in that case that lends some support to petitioner's contrary argument." Here are the subject headings of the government's brief in Great Northern. "The rightﾂｭofﾂｭway granted by the Act of March 3, 1875, is in the nature of an easement. The language of the 1875 Act shows that only an easement was granted. The legislative background and history of the 1875 Act show that the grant was of an easement rather than a fee. Subsequent administrative and congressional construction confirm that only an easement was granted." And the first sentence of the summary of the argument ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: There is also other portions of the brief. I would refer the Court, for instance, to Footnote 4 where the government said there would be a separate question raised about whether a patent holder subsequently who obtains the patent after the grant of rightﾂｭofﾂｭway would take this government interest. And the Court ﾂｭﾂｭ the government said in fact that would raise different questions. The Court recognized that on pages ﾂｭﾂｭ the last two pages of its opinion, it specifically limited its judgment to situations where the government retained interest in the entire surrounding parcel and, in fact, modified the judgment below which enjoined the railroad from oil drilling simply to apply to that. Not only that, the Court was writing on ﾂｭﾂｭ the government didn't cite, for instance ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Wait, wait. Before you go, you really think the Court was saying, It will be an easement when that suits the government, but it will be something else when that suits the government?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: No, I think what the Court was ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I mean, it's either an easement or it's not an easement. You really think the ﾂｭﾂｭ the opinion meant to reserve the question whether it was an easement.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: I do with respect to the issue that we have here. The question before the Court was whether the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway, which is a statutory term, Congress could have used "easement"; it could have used "fee." It did not. It uses a special statutory term which has accumulated meaning over time, particularly, in the railroad context. And in that context, the Court was deciding whether it was in the nature of an easement, which as our subject heading said, it's in the nature of an easement. But that's visﾂｭﾃﾂｭvis the United States ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I got that point.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: ﾂｭﾂｭ with respect to the mineral lands. And, in fact, the shift that, Justice Kagan, you were talking about is a shift about subsidies. It was a shift of subsidy away from these lavish land grants. And when Congress was stopping that ﾂｭﾂｭ that lavish grant to railroads, it made no sense to construe the act with respect to the mineral rights, which the Court said were ﾂｭﾂｭ were mineral riches. Why would Congress do that when it was cutting back on ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: That may be.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: But the term "easement" is a wellﾂｭknown term with an established meaning. And you're saying that ﾂｭﾂｭ you said it was an easement. The Court said it was an easement. You ﾂｭﾂｭ you persuaded the Court to say it was an easement. And now you're saying this is some kind of property right that has no name, previously unknown to the law.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: It's a rightﾂｭofﾂｭway. And, in fact, the government's brief also said that with respect ﾂｭﾂｭ and I'm quoting from page 9 ﾂｭﾂｭ with respect to the surface ﾂｭﾂｭ the control ﾂｭﾂｭ under any of the theories, even under the easement theory, which I don't believe is a true common law easement, the railroad's control of the surface was complete. And then we went on further ﾂｭﾂｭ and this is on pages 36 to 37 ﾂｭﾂｭ that said it was a fact that the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway has some of the attributes of a fee, and those included exclusive and perpetual occupation and remedies ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: All right. Let me put it this way.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: And so, again, if we were to rewrite our brief, we would certainly do so much more carefully now. But I think that the way that you read the brief is the way that you read opinions, which is in the context in which it was decided, particularly because Footnote 4, and this Court's opinion responding to Footnote 4, made clear that they were only addressing mineral rights where the United States held the surrounding parcel. And ﾂｭﾂｭ and that's particularly true because this Court had already construed Section 4 of the 1875 Act before Great Northern in Stalker and in Steinke. And in Stalker, the Court held that the subject to ﾂｭﾂｭ this is on page 154 ﾂｭﾂｭ a patent was subject to the railroad rights acquired by approval. That's the approval in Section 4, in the subject to language in Section 4. "Upon approval, the grounds so selected were segregated from the public lands and the required material to withdraw the land granted from the market." And then the Court goes on, and this is on page 154 again, "The later patent," the patent issued to a settler. Even though the local land office forgot to or omitted to mark the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway on the land plats, the subsequent patent was inoperative to pass title to those later patentholders. And then in Steinke ﾂｭﾂｭ this is 1922 ﾂｭﾂｭ the Court followed Stalker and said, "The approved map under Section 4 is the equivalent of a patent." And then they go on to say, citing Stalker, that the later patent was inoperative to pass title. And I think it's important, in both of those cases, the Court cited to Interior's regulations. In Stalker, the Court quoted at length the regulations from 1888, which, although the Court suggested that in Great Northern those were the first regulations, they actually weren't. There were prior regulations in 1878 which discuss nothing about the nature of the fee. And those, unfortunately, are hard to find. They are in Senate Executive Document 30, 45th Congress, Third Session, 1879. So ﾂｭﾂｭ but the regs that the Petitioners now rely on were specifically referenced by the Court, including the regs that say the disposition of the land surrounding the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway is subject to the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Yang, the Petitioners say that there are thousands or even tens of thousands of people in their position. Do you dispute that?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: We don't have good numbers, actually, on that.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: But what are the numbers? I mean, look, this is what I'm thinking. This is property law. It's not just a question of trying to work out what the case could or couldn't have stood for. If I try to remember my property class, it vaguely was ﾂｭﾂｭ which was a great class, A. James Casner, real expert. And he ﾂｭﾂｭ he, I think, said that when you convey subject of divestment, the ﾂｭﾂｭ something called season under the common law went to the recipient of the conveyance. But if you conveyed an easement, season did not pass. So in holding that it is an easement, you're holding there was no season, and therefore, it couldn't be a divestiture. Now, whether that's true or not or I've misremembered it, I'd go back to Justice Scalia's question. Is there a single example since the Domesday Book, since Dadonis, since the Bracton, on little to whatever it was, I mean, in the history of the common law up to the present, where a court has interpreted an ambiguous phrase, which doesn't say to grant an easement for one purpose, but not for the other. There may be some, but I didn't notice any cited in the government's brief.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: Well, I'm not sure that there would be any common law cases.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: No, I said in any case at all.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: But in this context ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Yes, yes. What is the case?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: In this context ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: What is the case? I'd like to read it.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: ﾂｭﾂｭ even with respect to the preﾂｭ1871 statutes, the Court would sometimes ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Oh, but they had ﾂｭﾂｭ they had a different view.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: No, no, no.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: They didn't say it was an easement.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: It ﾂｭﾂｭ they did.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I didn't read what ﾂｭﾂｭ it did?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: They sometimes referred to them as easements.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Which case?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: I don't have it in front of me. I know that ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Where do I ﾂｭﾂｭ where is it roughly in your brief?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: We didn't refer to those cases.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Oh, all right. Well ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: We might have cited the cases in our brief. We didn't refer to that part of the case.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I'm surprised that ﾂｭﾂｭ but the ﾂｭﾂｭ if you think there are some, I'll get my law clerks to look ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: But there are some, but the point was they were trying to discuss the nature of a statutory rightﾂｭofﾂｭway. Congress did not use the term "easement," it did not use the term "fee." And so the Court has been using common law terms which don't fit perfectly to describe certain kinds of ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Can you imagine or explain to me why a property lawyer worth his salt since 70 years ago or more, 1942, wouldn't have read that case and advised his client, who was buying the land, if the railroad abandons it, it's yours.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: Yes. There are ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Where is some evidence of that?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: There are at least four reasons.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: No, not reasons. I want to know if there's any authority, and then you get to the the reasons.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: Oh, yes. There's both statutory and case law authority. I would start with Stalker and Steinke, which specifically say that when you have approved the map, it's equivalent to a patent, and then therefore, any parcel subject ﾂｭﾂｭ is ﾂｭﾂｭ conveyed subject to the patent or the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway, which means you don't get any interest at least in the surface of the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway. So this ﾂｭﾂｭ those cases have never been overruled, one. Two, Congress in 1906, and this is ﾂｭﾂｭ this isn't significant. This is at page ﾂｭﾂｭ Section 940 at page 7 ﾂｭﾂｭ 6A and 7A of our brief. Congress said that each and every grant of rightﾂｭofﾂｭway under this 1875 Act ﾂｭﾂｭ each and every one ﾂｭﾂｭ "shall be declared forfeited if they haven't been constructed," and ﾂｭﾂｭ this is important ﾂｭﾂｭ "the United States resumes the full title to the lands covered thereby, free and discharged from such easement" ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Did they give compensation to the people ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: No.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: ﾂｭﾂｭ to whom they pronounced that?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: No.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: That can't change the meaning of the 1875 Act.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: But it reflects Congress's understanding of what the 1875 Act ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: It reflects a later Congress's understanding of what an earlier Congress did. We don't ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: Well, when we're ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: ﾂｭﾂｭ interpret statutes on the basis of what later Congresses think they meant.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: This Court did in Great Northern. In fact, this Court ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: People have done it.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: Okay. That's possible, but what we're talking about is Great Northern. And, in fact, Great Northern quotes this language of Section 940. The very next section goes on to say that the government's interests shall inure to the benefit of the land conveyed by the United States previously, which was subject to the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway. So that section itself shows that starting in 1906, and that's both 1906 and 1909, Congress understood that the government had a reversionary interest.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Yang, can I take you back to my question?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Thousands, tens of thousands? How many people are involved here and how many acres of land?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: Again, we don't know because the way that these things are disposed of, there has not been a centralized way of tracking it. It could be a significant amount, because the 1875 Act rightsﾂｭofﾂｭways were at least in number the most numerous. It's hard to know about mileage because the specific land grants were often quite long, but in terms ﾂｭﾂｭ there were at least a significant number of 1875 Act rights of ways. And so going back to Justice Breyer's question about why a good lawyer would not have great pause about saying that you got the surface interests. You have Stalker and Steinke. You have the Section 940, which was enacted in 1906 and 1909. You have Section 922 ﾂｭﾂｭ Section 912, which was enacted in 1922, which the Court has already discussed, which shows that whenever there is a forfeiture, the United States' interest reverts to first for roads and then to municipalities, and if there was anything left it would go to a land owner. That ﾂｭﾂｭ you know, was unquestioned it appears until ﾂｭﾂｭ at least with the surface interests until the midﾂｭ1980s, for 60 years. The municipalities and roads were ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, I see that they do and I certainly think bicycle paths are a good idea, but the problem that I see here is just what Justice Kagan is bringing up; that is, as I read this, I think there might be millions of acres in the last 70 years that have been conveyed. For all I know, there is some right of way that goes through people's houses ﾂｭﾂｭ you know, and all of a sudden, they are going to be living in their house and suddenly a bicycle will run through it, which isn't so bad, but I'm concerned about that, and your answer makes me more concerned ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: Well, I think it's telling ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: ﾂｭﾂｭ because you haven't suggested anything that makes me think there aren't millions and millions of acres involved. Now, what are you going to say that disabuses me of that?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: Well, I think it's telling that between 1922 and the midﾂｭ1980s this issue does not appear to have arisen. It has arisen only with respect to ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Exactly, because the lawyer would think ﾂｭﾂｭ when he reads the case of Great Northern, you think it's an easement.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: And then when the city takes the road and when the municipality ﾂｭﾂｭ excuse me ﾂｭﾂｭ when the municipality takes the right of way no one complained for 60 years? I mean, this is the background of the law. This is going to be ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: So how much is that? How much roughly is that? How often has it turned out that there was a conveyance ﾂｭﾂｭ after 1942 there was a conveyance of an abandoned right of way by the United States to land that had been previously been granted to a private owner?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: The amicus brief of ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: How often?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: Again, we don't have good numbers on how often.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I know you don't have good numbers, but can you give me a rough estimate?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: I think a significant amount of time, because the ﾂｭﾂｭ if you look at the amicus brief of the National Council on State Legislatures, they explain this. This is ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I will say ten times as much reliance on the one side than the other side, is that totally wrong?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: Your Honor, I can't speculate on numbers. I just don't have ﾂｭﾂｭ it would be not appropriate for me for the United States to speculate on numbers.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Yang, would you clarify what you're ﾂｭﾂｭ what Mr. Lechner told us. He said that to this very day the Department of Interior has taken the position that these railroad rights of way are easements.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: That's not correct because it needs to be qualified. After Great Northern, Interior has concluded in Amerada Hess, which we cite in our brief, that the subsurface interests would go to the patentee, but with respect to the surface interests, that has not been the case. In fact, until 1984 is the first case that I have seen in a case that let ﾂｭﾂｭ that was the counterpart to the Oregon Short Line case that we cite that led to the decision here. It just wasn't disputed about the surface interest.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Why then did no one from the Department of Interior join your brief?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: They agree with our brief. We don't always ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: They don't have a choice, do they? I mean, it's ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: No, we have been in close consultation with the Department of Interior and they have signed off on this brief. The agency that is at issue here is actually agriculture because agriculture is the forest service which has the lands. I think ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But both of them want more Federal lands, that's surprising, right, both agriculture and ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: I think that's maybe a little unfair, Justice Scalia. I think government is giving the Court its view of the law here. We are interpreting it. Multiple agencies have been consulted and we've been trying to come to grips with the Court's conflicting ﾂｭﾂｭ you know, language. If you take language in the abstract and you divorce it from the context of the case, the Court's decisions are conflicting, but when you look at the context of the case, you look at Great Northern with respect to saying that the subsurface interests, the mineral interests are like an easement with respect to the United States visﾂｭﾃﾂｭvis the railroad, but then you have Stalker and Steinke saying that when under Section 4 of the Act ﾂｭﾂｭ it's an authoritative construction of Section 4 of the Act. When you approve a map of a railroad under Section 4, it is tantamount to a patent and thereafter a subsequent patent does not confer an interest on the patentee. Those cases are reconciled, we think, by acknowledging that Stalker and Steinke control at least with respect to the surface interests, and then with respect to the subsurface interests, Great Northern clearly says that, at least visﾂｭﾃﾂｭvis the United States and the railroad where there is no thirdﾂｭparties involved that is deemed to be like an easement that would not give a subsurface interest. And there is real important reasons to distinguish between the subsurface and the surface with respect to rights of way. Surface for the right of way is what's important. It's critical. You need to have an artery that connects various parcels of land. That was true back when 1875 was enacted and it's true now with respect to highways and other uses that the government might put its land to.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: The patent here, Mr. Yang, which was, what, in the midﾂｭ1970s did not reserve anything. The government just made an uncritical grant to the grant family?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: And that was true both in Stalker and in Steinke with respect to the subsequent patents.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, I'm just suggesting that after Great Northern and then you're given this patent, which is unequivocal and does not reserve anything, why anybody would think that they hadn窶冲 gotten the whole ball of wax is a mystery.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: Well, I think you would have to ﾂｭﾂｭ I think if you had a good lawyer, the good lawyer would say, Look, we've got ﾂｭﾂｭ there is uncertainty here. You've got Stalker and Steinke, which says you get no interest. You have Great Northern, which doesn't address interest that pass to third parties. It only addresses the interest that the railroad has, and it says there is no mineral rights. And then you have Congress in 1906, 1909, 1922, and more recently in 1988, doubling down and saying ﾂｭﾂｭ you know, the United States' interest goes to roads first.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: It seems to me a fair reading of the history here, Mr. Yang, is that it really didn't occur to the government until very recently that these rights of way had value as anything other than railroad tracks, and indeed that the government was anxiously trying to give these things away because it thought that these spaghetti strips of land, it's of no use to the government, here, take them, get them off our hands. And having done that for many, many, many decades, the government faces a problem when it turns around and says, you know what, we forgot, there are bike paths.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: I don't think that's quite accurate. At least since 1922, the government has disposed of ﾂｭﾂｭ or Congress, I should say, has directed the government to dispose of its reversionary interests first to roads, and that remains true today. And then also from 1922 until 1988, it was supposed to go to any municipality, any land within a municipality goes to the municipality; and then third, it would go to any land owner of the surrounding parcel that was paying the interest subject to the right of way. Now, of course ﾂｭﾂｭ you know, Congress is free to choose to change its decision about how to dispose of U.S. Government interests to any government property. There is no vested interest in that. And I think what we are trying to say in our brief is that petitioner asks the Court essentially to nullify a significant ﾂｭﾂｭ significant enactments by Congress. It would nullify ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: That's true, but when you talk about ﾂｭﾂｭ I mean, I went and read ﾂｭﾂｭ when I read ﾂｭﾂｭ I thought your brief was very persuasive but then I read Great Northern and I thought they have ﾂｭﾂｭ they are really quoting it correctly. Here's what they said about the cases that you refer to like Stringer, The conclusion that the railroad was the owner of a limited fee ﾂｭﾂｭ which was the conclusion of those earlier cases ﾂｭﾂｭ was based on cases arising out of the 1871 Act, not the 1875 Act, and the change of policy in 1875 was brought ﾂｭﾂｭ was not brought to the Court's attention. And then they say that conclusion is inconsistent with the language of the 1875 Act. It's history. And it's early administrative interpretation. We therefore do not regard this earlier case as controlling.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: That was Townsend, which was dictum. We told the Court that Townsend was dictum. In Townsend, the dispute was by a railroad who got a decree from a State court saying it had the right of way.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, I thought they say in the Stringer case. Is that same as ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: Oh, I'm sorry, Stringer.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Yes, that's the one you were ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: No, no, we are not relying on ﾂｭﾂｭ we are relying on Stalker and Steinke, which are 1875 Act holdings. It's this Court construing the very statute that we're talking about, Section 4, which is the very section that the petitioner ﾂｭﾂｭ now, it would be kind of remarkable to read language in Great Northern which addressed a different question in which the government specifically reserved the question of what interest would transfer to a patentee. The Court specifically limited its decision so it wouldn't reach that issue. And then it sub silentio overruled two decisions by this Court on the very statute decided 30 years earlier when the Court was closer to the 1875 Act? That would be a pretty remarkable thing, I think. And what we're saying is that, no, Stalker and Steinke remain good law, even if you characterize the right of way as having attributes of an easement because it's clear it can't be a common law easement. I don't think that even ﾂｭﾂｭ petitioner may be able to dispute this ﾂｭﾂｭ but a common law easement, as this Court has explained, would not give as traditionally framed exclusive and perpetual occupation to the railroad. It would not give the remedy of the fee. All those are very practical, real rights that would have to be conferred that would be greater than a mere common law easement. And that's true back in 1871, and it's true in 1875, and we said it was true in our brief to this Court. We said as an easement ﾂｭﾂｭ you know, the fact that it has some of the attributes of a fee, including exclusive or perpetual occupation and the remedies of a fee and not an easement, doesn't take it out of the box of what we were talking about in that case. And so, again, I think you need to read it in context. I agree that if you just take Great Northern and look at it without peeling back the hood a little bit, there's language that ﾂｭﾂｭ you know, would lead ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: That's ﾂｭﾂｭ that's basically my problem. I guess you just don't have any specific things of what property lawyers were saying. But those two cases are from the era of Stringer, and at that point, apparently, the Court thought it was a limited fee.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: One was before ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: And then you get ﾂｭﾂｭ they were all ﾂｭﾂｭ was any of them after Great Northern?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: No, not after Great Northern.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: So ﾂｭﾂｭ so then in Great Northern it comes along?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: But Justice Breyer, I think it's a mistake to rely on these labels of limited fee and easement too strongly. That's what this Court's basically said in Union Pacific in 1957. In Union Pacific, the Court said look, these preﾂｭ1871 cases use the term "limited fee," but really, we're not going to deem that to be controlling with respect to the mineral rights. And the Court is doing exactly what we're saying should be done here, which is you understand the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway in the context of the statutory text, in the context of the environment, the surrounding statutory environment in the context of this Court's decisions, and it's not a binary blackﾂｭorﾂｭwhite choice between ﾂｭﾂｭ you know, common law terms. Congress didn't use those common law terms. Congress used the term "rightﾂｭofﾂｭway." It used the same text that it did in prior grants of rightsﾂｭofﾂｭway. And all we're trying to say is that there are real important interests here. It's not practically feasible to ever reconstruct these arteries through the lands. And they were important when Congress granted them to develop the lands in 1875, and they remain important today, as Congress has now repeatedly recognized in terms of granting public roads. So ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Do we know how many miles of public roads and how many miles of bike trails have been ﾂｭﾂｭ you don't know that either?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: On bike trails, this is ﾂｭﾂｭ this is not a RailsﾂｭtoﾂｭTrails Act case. There are different issues there. On the RailsﾂｭtoﾂｭTrails Act case ﾂｭﾂｭ you know, it's ﾂｭﾂｭ I don't have in mileage, but this is a trail that in this case the government used its property interest through the national forests as well as through this parcel for like a 22ﾂｭmile trail. There are probably 1,000 miles of trail that are ﾂｭﾂｭ that would be affected. And importantly, there's a lot of interest going forward that would be affected. When the law again ﾂｭﾂｭ the law of ﾂｭﾂｭ in 1922 was that the Congress would reserve the United States' interests for roads and municipalities and that was a significant ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Are there highways that would be affected? I mean, if we rule for the Brandts, are there suddenly going to be highways that can no longer be highways anymore?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: It certainly would call into question the legality of the land transfers. And I'm sure that could be something that could be litigated. There would be issues ﾂｭﾂｭ I'm sure defenses raised by the States and municipalities. But what the Court would be saying is that for ﾂｭﾂｭ since 1922, and this policy continues now, that the highways that have been created under 1875 Act would be invalid.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Can you think of anything I can do about this? It's a case, in my mind at least, where reliance interests on ﾂｭﾂｭ on a previous case in the property law area are important, and yet none of the briefs here really gives me a ﾂｭﾂｭ a reasonably concrete idea of how much reliance there has been. And ﾂｭﾂｭ and ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: On easements.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: So I'm somewhat in the dark.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: Yes. It's difficult to know, because again, these are kind of ad hoc things. When there's an abandonment, there might be proceedings, but there's been ﾂｭﾂｭ we've not been able to obtain centralized, reliable information on the total number, because this goes back a pretty long way. We're talking about, again, starting in 1922, you know. This has been unchallenged until we got two district court decisions in '80 ﾂｭﾂｭ in the mid '80s with respect to the surface. Now, on ﾂｭﾂｭ on the subsurface, Great Northern has ﾂｭﾂｭ you know, continued to be applied by Interior and that's been treated differently. And I think it's important also to remember that the regulations, not only were they cited by this Court in Stalker and Steinke, the Court was clearly aware of the regulations. In 1931, the ﾂｭﾂｭ a case called Otis Birch where Interior basically followed Stalker and recognized that you could not pass title. Now, that was a Mineral Act case, and the Court also ﾂｭﾂｭ Interior also said that because you can't separate the estates, or there's no reason to separate the surface from the subsurface, the mineral couldn't be transferred, either. That's been overtaken by events. It's important to recognize in '31, and when the regs were codified in the CFR in '38, which are the 1909 regs, which had never been changed or revisited on the nature of the interests, they simply just carried forward, even though Interior recognized that the ﾂｭﾂｭ there would be no interest given to patentees of the land after a rightﾂｭofﾂｭway is granted under Section 4 of the Act.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Maybe the reason you don't have records on this, which strikes me as pretty unusual that the government doesn't know what it owns, is that for decades, you didn't think that you owned a reversionary interest.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: Well, for decades, we thought we owned a reversionary interest. It's just that ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: But you don't have any records on where these things are.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: Well, but Congress told us ﾂｭﾂｭ told everyone how to dispose of it. It went to streets, roads, highways, then to municipalities, and if then not municipalities, it would go to the landowner. So the United States ﾂｭﾂｭ you know, disposed of its reversionary interests through statute. It wasn't until 1988 that the policy changed to ﾂｭﾂｭ to prioritize highways and then the U.S.'s interests after that. So it's understandable why we haven't been intimately involved with all these forfeitures that have occurred over time. Congress directed how the United States interests should be disposed of.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: What would ﾂｭﾂｭ what would be the exposure of the United States if we ﾂｭﾂｭ if you loseﾂｭﾂｭ are there takings claims now?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: There are takings claims also that would ﾂｭﾂｭ any 1875 Act case or rightﾂｭofﾂｭway which has been abandoned, which has then put to another use, whether it be a highway under the current Section 912, or whether it reverts to the United States, or even if, for instance, it doesn't revert to the United States ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Are there any statute of limitations? Would all of this be subject to the APA, or ﾂｭﾂｭ or a claim for damages against the U.S.?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: I ﾂｭﾂｭ I don't believe the APA would govern a claim of damages against the U.S. There are takings claims which ﾂｭﾂｭ which proceed under the Tucker Act against the U.S. Or the Little Tucker Act, depending on the amount. That wouldn't be an APA issue.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: What about adverse possession? Can't the ﾂｭﾂｭ can't the ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: Adverse possession is certainly an issue that might ﾂｭﾂｭ would come up.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I would think if there's been no objection to these spaghetti strips of land, I don't think condemnation would cost the government a whole lot for that matter. What, you know ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: Well, we've faced ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: ﾂｭﾂｭ what a spaghetti strip of land through ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: We faced a very considerable amount of litigation in recent years.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Doesn't Interior or some ﾂｭﾂｭ I mean, doesn't the highway ﾂｭﾂｭ aren't there people in the government who keep track of where the highways are built?

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: Well, for local roads? This is ﾂｭﾂｭ the idea that these ﾂｭﾂｭ these rightsﾂｭofﾂｭways would go to States and localities, not Federal highways. That's ﾂｭﾂｭ that's Section 912.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Yes, but you should ﾂｭﾂｭ should know how much land the United States owns. It's ﾂｭﾂｭ it's incredible that ﾂｭﾂｭ that there's no record in the Interior Department or anywhere else of what land the United States own. You claim you own these thousands of acres, and you say we've not kept track of it. We just know where it's going to go, but we don't know what we own.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: There are some records that you can get from archives with respect to specific things, but it's not centralized. We don't have a way of aggregating what has happened over the last 90 years. That's the difficulty that we have here.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel.

turns.name: YANG
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Mr. Lechner, you have 4 minutes remaining.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: We do know how many acres are involved in this case, and the government is claiming 10 of the 83 acres that it patented to Marvin Brandt's parents. As this Court has noted, stare decisis concerns are ﾂｭﾂｭ in many cases involving property rights. With respect to Stalker and Steinke, those were preﾂｭGreat Northern; those were limited fee cases. At most, they stand for first in time, first in right. Steinke was written by Justice Van Devanter who also wrote Stringham, which Great Northern overturned. And that's why Stalker and Steinke fell out of use over the years. And if Stalker and Steinke were so important, the government should have brought those cases to the Court's attention in Great Northern, but it didn't. With respect to the 1931 land decision the government just brought up, I ﾂｭﾂｭ I refer the Court to solicitor's opinion ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I guess it would be the private parties that would have brought it up in Great Northern, wouldn't it? And do you have any light to shed on the comparative amounts? I mean, they're saying ﾂｭﾂｭ for all we know there are billions of dollars worth of takings claims that will come out where the highways run, and you're arguing, of course, any reasonable property lawyer would have relied on Great Northern to think it was just conveying an easement. But ﾂｭﾂｭ you know, I'm not a property lawyer, and so what actually happened matters. And the amounts matter, at least to me. And do you have any light to shed?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: I don't have any light to shed on the actual mileage. We know it's thousands. We know that ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: What about on your side? What about the possibility that millions of acres has been conveyed and there are tens of thousands or hundreds of thousands of abandoned railroads that property lawyers thought went to the person who bought them, and run through somebody's house? I mean, is that a figment of my imagination?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: And you would say no, it isn't, and therefore I'm asking you is there any empirical support anywhere for how property lawyers have treated Great Northern, how much was conveyed, et cetera? Anything you can say on that would be helpful.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: I have nothing to say about the quantity, but I know that with S.C. ﾂｭﾂｭ the Seventh Circuit ruled in S.C. Johnson that the title insurance thought that was a landmark decision because it resolved a lot of problems with the title insurance industry. Each mile of the rightﾂｭofﾂｭway takes up 24 acres. At one point there were 270,000, as this Court noted in Preseault, there was 270,000 miles of roads at the peak in the early 1920s and about 130 of those had been abandoned by the time of the Preseault decision. I want to also address the forfeiture provision that they mention and Great Northern relied on Section 43, 940, to confirm its conclusion that the S and D, these are easements because 43 U.S.C. 940 calls them an easement. And with respect to land that the government still owned at the time of abandonment, they said that the government's land would be discharged of the easements.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Is there any doctrine in property law that if a right of access is granted and its to the exclusion of all other uses, it's ﾂｭﾂｭ it looks for all purposes like absolute control, that it ceases to be an easement and becomes a limited fee? I mean, is there some magic that takes place in property law so that if there's a grant that conveys such total control, that it construed not to be an easement?

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: I don't know of any.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: I've never seen it.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Roads, highways are conveyed ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Have you even heard of the term "limited fee" until this case? I never heard it.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Well, I read these ﾂｭﾂｭ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: A. James Casner didn't talk to me about limited fee.

turns.name: LECHNER
turns.text: Well, I read these cases in law school so I was aware of the term. Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
